title: Chapter 18: Node 4
content: They found it on the twelfth day.

The cipher in Meilin's journal translated to three frequency coordinates that corresponded to positions within the estate's leyline grid. The third position—hidden behind the oldest wing's eastern foundation, in a sub-basement that predated the current structure by centuries—produced a resonance match when Xue channeled her mother's signature frequency.

The sub-basement was accessed through a supply closet in the servants' quarters—a door so ordinary that no one had thought to investigate it in generations. Behind the door was a staircase carved from living rock, descending into darkness that even Ren's enhanced sight couldn't fully penetrate.
At the bottom was a chamber.

It was small—four meters by four, with walls of bare stone covered in pre-dynastic inscriptions that glowed faintly in the color-that-wasn't-a-color of the containment array. In the center of the chamber, suspended in a cradle of hardened light, was Node 4.
A jade tablet.

Not a pendant, not a bell—*a tablet*, the size of a hardcover book, inscribed on both faces with the containment array's maintenance protocols. It was beautiful in a way that Ren's enhanced senses could perceive but his mortal eyes couldn't fully translate: a three-dimensional frequency map rendered in jadeite, each inscription a note in a song that had been playing since before recorded history.
The Salvation System:

`NODE 4: JADE TABLET (Containment mode — ACTIVE)`
`STATUS: Fully sealed by Liang Meilin's bloodline frequency`
`CONTAINMENT INTEGRITY: 94% (degradation minimal — this node was NEVER converted)`
`NOTE: This node is the most intact of the seven. Liang Meilin hid it before the Patriarch could convert it. If we can keep it in containment mode, the portal cannot fully activate regardless of the other six nodes.`
*The portal cannot fully activate regardless of the other six nodes.*
Ren read the sentence three times. The implications cascaded through his strategic mind like dominoes.

If Node 4 remained in containment mode, the seven-pointed star would always be incomplete. The portal couldn't open with a missing piece. The Patriarch could convert all six remaining nodes and it wouldn't matter—Node 4 was the keystone, and the keystone was *still locked*.
"We don't need to re-seal the other nodes," Ren breathed. "We just need to keep this one safe."
Xue turned from the tablet, her face illuminated by its faint glow. "My mother knew. She hid the one node he needed most."
"She was an architect. She understood structural weakness. The seven-pointed star requires all seven vertices—remove one, and the geometry collapses. Node 4 is the keystone."
"Then my father needs it." Xue's voice was deadly calm. "And it's in my bloodline vault, keyed to my frequency. He can't take it without me."

The power dynamic had just inverted. For twelve years, Xue had been a pawn in her father's game—the innocent bloodline carrier, useful only for her genetic inheritance and her legal function as the firewall anchor. Now she was the guardian of the single most important asset in the portal project.
The jade pendant at her throat was Node 3—a converted node, compromised, but still in her possession. The jade tablet was Node 4—still locked, still functional, still holding the line.
Two of seven nodes. In one woman's hands.

"The Patriarch knows you're visiting your mother's room," Ren said, pivoting to threat assessment. "He doesn't know what you found—not yet. But he's monitoring you more closely. The eastern intelligence access he gave me includes a surveillance feed on your schedule."
"My own father is spying on me."
"Your father has been spying on everyone since before you were born. The question is how long before he realizes that you've found Node 4."
"We need to secure it." Xue reached toward the tablet—but the cradle of hardened light flared when her fingers approached, and she pulled back instinctively.

"It's keyed to your bloodline signature," Ren said. "Only you can release it. But releasing it means it becomes mobile—vulnerable. Right now, it's protected by your mother's seal and the chamber's array. If you take it out, your father will sense the seal breaking."

"Then I don't release it. I reinforce it." Xue closed her eyes, and the jade pendant at her throat began to glow—the channeling frequency she'd spent twelve days mastering, extended now toward the tablet's containment cradle. "My mother's seal is strong, but it's been degrading for twelve years without maintenance. If I can reinforce it with my own frequency—"

"Don't. Not yet." Ren stepped forward, touching her arm. "If you reinforce the seal, your father will detect the channeling. The mansion's array monitors for bloodline activity in the lower levels. He'll know someone accessed the chamber."
Xue opened her eyes. "So we leave it."

"We leave it. For now. It's safer here than anywhere else—no one knows about this chamber except us and your mother's ghost. The seal will hold for months. We come back when we're ready, when your father's attention is focused elsewhere, and we reinforce it then."
"And the other nodes?"
"The Bronze Bell is drifting toward containment. If we can access the Patriarch's Seal—Node 2—we can corrupted its conversion as well. And your pendant—"
"Node 3." Her hand touched the jade at her throat. "It's been converted. Can it be re-sealed?"

"In theory. But you're wearing it. The conversion process has bound it to your bloodline signature in its current state. Re-sealing it would require undoing that binding, which means removing it from your person for several hours while we recalibrate." He paused. "Removing it would also weaken the causal firewall that's keeping you—that's keeping *us*—alive."
"How much would it weaken?"

"Enough that your father could detect the change. Enough that the entity beyond the breach could sense the gap. Not enough to trigger the portal—but enough to announce that we're tampering with the seal."

"Then we wait. While the Bell drifts, while I train, while we gather intelligence." Xue looked at the tablet one more time—the keystone, her mother's last gift, the single object standing between the Patriarch and the end of the world. "How long do we have?"

"The Patriarch has Node 6 arriving from Tokyo in three days. Once integrated, that's five of seven. He'll accelerate the search for Node 7—and he'll start looking harder at you. The marriage, the pendant, your recent trips to your mother's room. He's not stupid, Xue."
"No. He's not." She turned toward the staircase. "We need a distraction. Something big enough to pull his attention away from me while we work."

"I might have one." Ren followed her up the stairs. "The Zhao family. They're the Patriarch's financial partners, but the partnership is built on lies. The Zhao elder doesn't know the full scope of the portal project—he thinks he's investing in dimensional energy extraction, not apocalypse. If he finds out the truth..."

"The alliance fractures. And my father loses his funding and human infrastructure." Xue's lips curved—not quite a smile, but the expression of a strategist seeing a board open up. "How do we tell him?"
"I'm working on it."
Three days later, the Node 6 retrieval team returned from Tokyo.

The jade tablet was smaller than the Bronze Bell—palm-sized, obsidian-black, inscribed with silver runes that seemed to move in peripheral vision. It was carried through the western loading bay in a sealed case, flanked by four armed guards and one Stage 3 spore host.

Zhao Mingyue looked different up close. Ren watched the convoy's arrival from a second-floor window, his enhanced sight catching details that distance should have obscured: the too-smooth skin, the micro-oscillations in her pupils, the way her fingers twitched in patterns that matched the dimensional array's pulse. She was a puppet—beautiful, functional, and completely hollow.
The Patriarch met the convoy at the loading bay. Ren watched the old man take the sealed case from Mingyue's hands with the tenderness of a father receiving a child.
*He doesn't see her as a person,* Ren realized. *She's a tool to him. Like Feng. Like everyone.*
The case disappeared into the restricted wing. Node 6 was now inside the estate, entering its calibration cycle. Five of seven nodes in the Patriarch's control.
And the keystone was in a sub-basement, guarded by a dead woman's seal and her daughter's determination.
The countdown in Ren's peripheral vision pulsed:
`1072 days`
But the calculus had changed. With Node 4 secured, the portal couldn't fully activate. The Patriarch could convert six nodes and still fail—because the keystone was locked.

*He'll figure it out eventually,* the Salvation System warned. *When Node 7 is acquired, he'll attempt the full activation sequence, and it will fail. Then he'll know one of the nodes is compromised. He'll search the entire estate until he finds it.*
*Which means we have—*
Ren calculated. Node 7 was still missing. The Patriarch hadn't located it. At the current rate of acquisition, finding the seventh node could take months—or it could happen tomorrow.
*We have until he finds Node 7. After that, he'll start looking for the missing piece.*
One thousand seventy-two days.
